01_initial_request.user_intent: To develop a fast and sensitive computational approach for direct peptide identification from DIA data
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: Processing speed and peptide identification sensitivity

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] DIA data analysis requires peptide identification from complex spectra. [Gap] Existing tools like DIA-Umpire are time-consuming due to untargeted feature detection. [Decision] To achieve fast and sensitive identification, chose to use MSFragger-DIA to perform direct database search on DIA MS/MS spectra prior to feature detection, expecting to obtain a list of peptide candidates.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: List of peptide candidates per spectrum with calibrated mass errors and extracted ion chromatograms.

references.relevance: Describes the initial release and implementation of MSFragger-DIA.
Describes the fragment ion indexing-based search engine leveraged by MSFragger-DIA.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Initial search scores may not be optimal for FDR estimation. [Gap] Need to improve peptide identification confidence. [Decision] To enhance scoring, chose to use MSBooster to perform deep-learning-based score calculation on MSFragger-DIA output, expecting to obtain additional predictive scores.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Enhanced peptide-spectrum match scores using deep learning features.

references.relevance: Describes the deep learning-based feature improvement tool.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Scores need to be calibrated for error probability estimation. [Gap] Raw scores do not provide posterior error probabilities. [Decision] To calculate error probabilities, chose to use Percolator to perform rescoring on MSBooster output, expecting to obtain posterior error probabilities (PEP).
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Peptide-spectrum matches with posterior error probabilities.

references.relevance: Describes the semi-supervised learning tool for peptide identification.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Peptide matches need to be inferred to proteins. [Gap] Peptide level data does not directly quantify proteins. [Decision] To infer proteins, chose to use ProteinProphet to perform protein inference on Percolator output, expecting to obtain protein groups.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Protein groups inferred from peptide matches.

references.relevance: Describes the statistical model for protein identification.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Identified peptides and proteins need false discovery rate control. [Gap] Raw identifications contain false positives. [Decision] To control errors, chose to use Philosopher to perform FDR filtering on ProteinProphet output, expecting to obtain filtered identifications at 1% FDR.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Filtered peptide and protein identifications with controlled FDR.

references.relevance: Describes the toolkit for shotgun proteomics data analysis and FDR filtering.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] A spectral library is needed for targeted quantification. [Gap] Identified peptides need to be organized into a library. [Decision] To build the library, chose to use EasyPQP to perform spectral library building on filtered identifications, expecting to obtain a target spectral library.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Spectral library containing peptide ions, RT, and fragment intensities.

references.relevance: Describes the spectral library building module integrated in FragPipe.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Peptide ions need to be quantified from primary DIA data. [Gap] Identification alone does not provide abundance. [Decision] To extract quantification, chose to use DIA-NN to perform targeted extraction using the EasyPQP library, expecting to obtain quantified precursors and proteins.

parameters.input_data: Primary DIA data and Spectral Library

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Quantified precursors, peptides, and proteins from DIA runs.

references.relevance: Describes the neural network-based quantification tool.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The workflow needs validation on staggered-window DIA data. [Gap] Performance on GPF-DIA and staggered windows is unknown. [Decision] To evaluate sensitivity, chose to use MSFragger-DIA to analyze 2018-HeLa and 2020-Yeast datasets, expecting to obtain quantified peptide counts and CVs.
02_agent_trajectory.observation: Highest sensitivity among compared tools (FP-MSF demonstrated the highest sensitivity), low CVs similar to DIA-NN in silico library.

references.relevance: Source of the staggered-window DIA datasets used for benchmarking.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The workflow needs validation on low-input and single-cell data. [Gap] Sensitivity on low sample amounts is critical. [Decision] To evaluate low-input sensitivity, chose to use MSFragger-DIA to analyze low-input-cell (0.75 ng, 7.5 ng) and single-cell (1 cell, 117 cells) datasets, expecting to obtain quantified protein counts.

parameters.input_data: Low-input-cell (Siyal et al.) and Single-cell (Gebreyesus et al.)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: FP-MSF and DIA-NN in silico library-based pipelines have similar sensitivities higher than others; FP-MSF hybrid quantified more proteins in 1-cell experiment.

references.citation: Siyal et al., 2021 and Gebreyesus et al., 2022
references.relevance: Source of the low-input and single-cell datasets.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The workflow needs validation on phosphoproteomics data. [Gap] Performance on PTM-enriched data and computational speed needs assessment. [Decision] To evaluate PTM capability and speed, chose to use MSFragger-DIA to analyze melanoma-phospho dataset, expecting to obtain phosphopeptide counts and runtime.

parameters.modifications: Phosphorylation of serine, threonine, and tyrosine

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Similar numbers of quantified phosphopeptides; FragPipe with MSFragger-DIA had the fastest overall speed (at least six times faster than DIA-NN in silico library-based).

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] The workflow needs validation on large-scale clinical data. [Gap] Scalability and correlation with other omics layers need assessment. [Decision] To evaluate scale and quality, chose to use MSFragger-DIA to analyze ccRCC cohort (187 DIA runs), expecting to obtain protein quantification and correlation metrics.

parameters.input_data: ccRCC cohort (187 single-injection DIA runs, 8 fractionated DDA runs)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Quantified ~7000 proteins without DDA, small increase with DDA; FP-MSF pipeline at least two times faster than DIA-NN in silico library-based, five to ten times faster than MaxDIA.

references.relevance: Source of the large-scale clinical proteomics dataset.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Data quality needs comparative evaluation against RNA-seq and TMT data. [Gap] Need to ensure DIA quantification is biologically consistent. [Decision] To evaluate data quality, chose to use OmicsEV to perform quality control on ccRCC quantification tables, expecting to obtain PCA plots and correlation metrics.

parameters.input_data: DIA protein quantification tables, RNA-seq data, TMT DDA data

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Tumor and normal samples well separated; DIA and TMT DDA-based protein quantification data have similar gene-wise correlations with RNA abundances.

references.relevance: Describes the tool for quality evaluation of omics data tables.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Benchmarking against state-of-the-art DIA tools (Spectronaut, DIA-NN, MaxDIA, DIA-Umpire) across multiple datasets (Benchmark, Staggered-windows, Low-input, Single-cell, Phosphoproteomics, Large-scale ccRCC)

processing_speed.value: 6x faster than DIA-NN in silico (Phospho); 5-10x faster than MaxDIA (ccRCC)
processing_speed.interpretation: MSFragger-DIA demonstrates significantly faster processing speed compared to competing pipelines.

identification_sensitivity.interpretation: MSFragger-DIA based workflows resulted in the most quantified precursors in benchmark and staggered-window experiments, and quantified more proteins in 1-cell experiments.

fdr_control.interpretation: All tools control false positives well; MSFragger-DIA maintains accurate FDR estimation using E. coli spike-in controls.

quantification_precision.interpretation: MSFragger-DIA yielded similarly low CVs to DIA-NN in silico library, indicating comparable quantification precision.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The research objective was achieved. MSFragger-DIA successfully provides a fast and sensitive computational approach for direct peptide identification from DIA data, outperforming or matching state-of-the-art tools in sensitivity while offering superior processing speed across various sample types and acquisition schemes.
